patches.find: WIN-010 / PRED-R002: The dome predicts the August 12, 2026 solar eclipse will produce a magnetic anomaly of −17 to −21 nT at Ebro Observatory (Spain), with scaled predictions for seven stations based on eclipse coverage percentage. The prediction is registered with a git SHA timestamp (2026-03-22). This is presented as the dome's strongest prospective test.
patches.replace: WIN-010 / PRED-R002: The dome predicts the August 12, 2026 solar eclipse will produce a magnetic anomaly at 9 named INTERMAGNET stations, with per-station Field Strength Factors (FSF) ranging from 0.642 to 2.075 and two separate baselines: the BOU baseline (−10.9 nT) produces station predictions from −5.0 to −12.8 nT, and the W004 baseline (−22.24 nT) produces predictions from −10.3 to −26.2 nT. The dome's homepage summarizes this as “−17 to −21 nT at Ebro.” The full prediction spans approximately −5 to −26 nT with error bars across both baselines. The prediction is registered with git SHA timestamps and OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring (2026-03-22). This is presented as the dome's strongest prospective test. See Section 3.2 for the full dual-baseline structure and discriminating test analysis.
patches.rationale: The section described the eclipse prediction as a simple single-range claim (-17 to -21 nT, seven stations, coverage scaling). The actual registered prediction is a dual-baseline system with 9 stations and station-specific FSF values. The homepage still shows -17 to -21 nT as a simplified summary but the full structure is more complex. Also corrects seven to nine stations.

patches.find: This test is addressed in detail in Section 3.2 (Eclipse 2026 Page). In summary: the dome claims to predict the August 12, 2026 solar eclipse path across the Iberian Peninsula, but the prediction is derived from the same Besselian element calculations (JPL DE440/441 ephemerides) used by every eclipse predictor. The dome's local-sun geometry does not independently produce eclipse paths — the site uses standard ephemeris data and claims the result. This is not a discriminating test.
patches.replace: This test is addressed in detail in Section 3.2. The dome registers a magnetic anomaly prediction for the August 12, 2026 eclipse: −17 to −21 nT at Ebro (summarized from a dual-baseline system spanning −5 to −26 nT across 9 INTERMAGNET stations). The eclipse’s geographic path is defined by standard Besselian element calculations (JPL DE440/441 ephemerides) — the dome’s local-sun geometry does not independently produce eclipse paths. For the magnetic anomaly prediction itself, the Chapman ionospheric mechanism (peer-reviewed since 1933) predicts 5–25 nT under identical conditions, making the prediction non-discriminating. See Section 3.2 for the full analysis.
patches.rationale: Kill-Shot Test 5 incorrectly described the dome's claim as being about eclipse PATH prediction, when Section 3.2 analyzes the MAGNETIC ANOMALY prediction. The cross-reference anchor was also wrong (was #part3, should be #eclipse-analysis). Updated to correctly frame both aspects: the path uses standard Besselian elements, while the magnetic anomaly prediction is non-discriminating because the Chapman mechanism predicts the same range.

patches.find: The eclipse test states the globe predicts "0.0 nT exactly" when peer-reviewed literature documents 5–20 nT perturbations via the Chapman mechanism.
patches.replace: The eclipse test implies the globe predicts essentially no magnetic eclipse effect, when peer-reviewed literature documents 5–20 nT perturbations via the Chapman mechanism (Chapman 1933; Meza et al. 2021).
patches.rationale: The verbatim quote '0.0 nT exactly' could not be verified on the dome site and the wording may not exist verbatim. The dome's Kill-Shot Test 5 frames the globe as predicting no effect, but we should not attribute a specific quoted phrase that we cannot confirm. Changed to 'implies the globe predicts essentially no magnetic eclipse effect' which is accurate to the dome's framing without asserting an unverified verbatim quote.

patches.find: But monitor.py computes ΔB = −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −29.1 nT. The published range and the computational model disagree by ~40%. Which prediction is being tested?
patches.replace: But based on code analysis of the dome’s repository (see Part 4.6), monitor.py appears to compute ΔB = −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −29.1 nT, where 1.672 is KAPPA — the magnetic-gravity coupling constant from WIN-012 — rather than the registered station-specific FSF values (0.642–2.075). The monitoring code that will judge pass/fail does not implement the prediction as registered. The published range and the computational implementation appear to disagree by ~40%. Which prediction is being tested?
patches.rationale: ISS-429: The claim that monitor.py computes the specific formula was unverified since monitor.py is not publicly archived for independent inspection. Added hedge 'based on code analysis of the dome's repository' and 'appears to compute' to indicate this is based on prior code review rather than a live source. Also added the KAPPA identification (1.672 = WIN-012 coupling constant) which was missing from this occurrence, and clarified what FSF values should have been used.

patches.find: A further internal inconsistency: the dome's monitor.py computes the eclipse prediction as −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −28.9 nT, using KAPPA (the magnetic-gravity coupling constant from WIN-012) as the field strength factor. But the dome's predictions page lists station-specific FSF values ranging from 0.642 to 2.075, derived from "V12 H(r)/r dome geometry." The monitoring infrastructure uses a different formula from the registered predictions — the code that will judge pass/fail does not implement the prediction it is supposed to test.
patches.replace: A further internal inconsistency uncovered during code analysis of the dome’s repository (Part 4.6): monitor.py appears to compute the eclipse prediction as −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −28.9 nT, using KAPPA (the magnetic-gravity coupling constant from WIN-012) as the field strength factor rather than the registered per-station FSF values (0.642–2.075). But the dome’s predictions page lists station-specific FSF values ranging from 0.642 to 2.075, derived from "V12 H(r)/r dome geometry." The monitoring infrastructure appears to use a different formula from the registered predictions — the code that will judge pass/fail may not implement the prediction as registered.
patches.rationale: Added hedge 'uncovered during code analysis' and 'appears to compute' since monitor.py is not publicly archived for independent verification. Also clarified that station-specific FSF values (0.642-2.075) should have been used instead of KAPPA.

patches.find: E-PRED-B (Hartland exceeds Ebro despite lower coverage) is the only one not trivially predicted by standard ionospheric physics. However, the dual-baseline structure covers nearly the entire physically plausible quiet-day eclipse signal space
patches.replace: E-PRED-B (Hartland exceeds Ebro despite lower coverage) is the only one not trivially predicted by standard ionospheric physics, and we acknowledge it as a genuine discriminating sub-test: if Hartland’s anomaly exceeds Ebro’s in a pattern that cannot be explained by Hartland’s latitude-dependent Sq current geometry or local ionospheric conditions, this would be meaningful evidence for the dome’s FSF-from-H(r)/r claim. However, standard ionospheric factors (station latitude, E-region electron density, proximity to the Sq vortex focus) independently produce station-to-station variation, so E-PRED-B alone would not be conclusive. Setting that aside, the dual-baseline structure covers nearly the entire physically plausible quiet-day eclipse signal space
patches.rationale: ISS-430: SEC-6.2 mentioned E-PRED-B but did not explicitly acknowledge it as a genuine discriminating test. The curmudgeon correctly flagged that E-PRED-B is the dome's strongest sub-test (Hartland > Ebro geometry is specifically derived from H(r)/r, not trivially predicted by standard coverage scaling). Added explicit acknowledgment that E-PRED-B is a genuine test while noting that standard ionospheric factors complicate interpretation.

patches.find:
Refuted by Data: {{TALLY_REFUTED}} |
Standard Model Explains: {{TALLY_STD}} |
Self-Contradicted: {{TALLY_SELFCON}} |
Misleading: {{TALLY_MISLEADING}} |
Not Demonstrated: {{TALLY_NOTDEMO}} |
Unfalsifiable: {{TALLY_UNFALSIFIABLE}}
patches.replace:
Self-Contradicted: {{TALLY_SELFCON}} |
Refuted by Data: {{TALLY_REFUTED}} |
Not Demonstrated: {{TALLY_NOTDEMO}} |
Standard Model Explains: {{TALLY_STD}} |
Misleading: {{TALLY_MISLEADING}} |
Unfalsifiable: {{TALLY_UNFALSIFIABLE}}
patches.rationale: ISS-447: Verdict order was arbitrary. Reordered by evidential force: (1) Wrong on Own Terms first (Self-Contradicted = dome refutes itself, Refuted by Data = external contradictions), (2) Not Evidenced (Not Demonstrated, Standard Model Explains), (3) Rhetorical quality issues (Misleading), (4) Untestable (Unfalsifiable). Self-Contradicted is most damaging because it requires no external data.

patches.find: Not Demonstrated: {{TALLY_NOTDEMO}} (built on unconfirmed data, post-hoc curve fits, or circular derivations; the proposed mechanism is asserted but not independently verified)
patches.replace: Not Demonstrated: {{TALLY_NOTDEMO}} (built on unconfirmed data, post-hoc curve fits, or circular derivations; the proposed mechanism is asserted but not independently verified; also includes claims adopted from external experimental sources without any derivation from dome geometry)
patches.rationale: ISS-448: The Not Demonstrated definition covered 'unreplicated fringe experiments' and post-hoc fits, but did not explicitly include cases where the dome simply adopts an external claim (e.g., Meyl scalar waves, El Gordo cluster) and labels it a 'confirmed prediction' without deriving it from dome parameters. Added this case to the definition.
